title: Building a Sampling Distribution of p̂

steps.instruction: A large bowl of beads is **60% red** (a "success"). We can't see the whole bowl in real life, but here we can — that's the **Population** panel, with the true proportion marked at **p = 0.60**. We'll scoop out samples of **n = 40** beads at a time and track the proportion red in each scoop.
steps.observe: Find **p = 0.60** marked on the Population panel. Each scoop of 40 beads will rarely be exactly 60% red — that sample-to-sample wobble is what we're about to study.

steps.instruction: **Before you draw anything, commit to a prediction.** Imagine taking *hundreds* of scoops of 40 beads and plotting each scoop's proportion red.

gate.question: Where will those sample proportions (p̂) pile up?

choices.feedback: You've committed — now draw samples and see whether the pile-up lands where you expected.
You've committed — draw samples and check. Watch where the center of the pile actually falls.
You've committed — draw samples and see whether the proportions really spread out evenly, or cluster somewhere.

steps.instruction: Click **+1** once. Watch the **One sample** panel show your scoop of 40 beads, collapse to a single proportion **p̂**, and drop that one dot into the **Sampling distribution** panel at the bottom.
steps.observe: That bottom panel is a **distribution of statistics** — one dot per sample, not per bead. Click **+1** a few more times to add more dots.

steps.instruction: Now click **+1000** to take a thousand scoops quickly and let the sampling distribution fill in.

requires.hint: Click **+1000** to draw at least 500 samples, then answer below.

steps.observe: Look at the bottom panel and the **SD of p̂'s** readout, then compare it to the theory value **√(p(1−p)/n)**.

gate.question: Where is the sampling distribution of p̂ centered, and is it bell-shaped?
gate.feedback: It centers at p = 0.60 (the true proportion) and is roughly normal because np = 24 and n(1−p) = 16 are both ≥ 10. Your **{{samples}}** samples gave an SD of p̂'s of **{{statSD}}** — very close to the theory value √(p(1−p)/n) = **{{theorySE}}**.
gate.retryFeedback: Look at where the dots pile up relative to the 0.60 line, and whether the shape is mound-shaped or flat.

steps.instruction: Two different things change this picture in two different ways. First click **+1000 again** (more samples, same n). Then change **sample size n to 100** and draw **+1000** — or click **Freeze this distribution to compare** first, then change n, so you can see both at once.

gate.question: Which change made the distribution narrower?
gate.feedback: Only larger n narrows it. More samples just fill in the *same* distribution more smoothly — its spread doesn't change. Larger n produces a *different*, narrower distribution, because √(p(1−p)/n) shrinks as n grows.
gate.retryFeedback: Compare the frozen n = 40 distribution to the n = 100 one — same center, different width. Then recall what adding more dots at a fixed n did to the width.

steps.instruction: You've built the sampling distribution of a proportion from scratch.
steps.reveal: The sample proportion p̂ varies from sample to sample, but predictably: its distribution is centered at the true p, has standard deviation √(p(1−p)/n), and is approximately normal when np and n(1−p) are both at least about 10. Crucially, **more samples** sharpen the picture of that distribution, while **a larger n** changes it — shrinking the spread. This is the engine behind every confidence interval and test for a proportion.
